coredata.dc:title: Hybrid carbon dots platform enabling opportunities for desired optical properties and redox characteristics by-design
coredata.dc:creator: LeCroy, Gregory E.
Wang, Ping
Bunker, Christopher E.
Shiral Fernando, K.A.
Liang, Weixiong
Ge, Lin
Reibold, McIver
Sun, Ya-Ping
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDots) exploit unique properties of small carbon nanoparticles, which represent the often under-appreciated zero-dimensional carbon allotrope. The carbon nanoparticles in CDots could be modified by a selected organic chromophore of desired strong red/near-IR absorption in the dot synthesis to obtain a specific type of hybrid CDots. The reported results show that these hybrid CDots derived from the modification of core carbon nanoparticles by selected dyes or other chromophoric species represent a highly versatile and effective platform for CDots of desired optical properties and photoinduced redox characteristics in a by-design fashion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Charge transfers
Zero-dimensional carbon allotrope
Optoelectronics

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from Air Force Office of Scientific Research (AFOSR) through the program of Dr. Kenneth Caster and Air Force Research Laboratory is gratefully acknowledged. M.R. was a participant of Palmetto Academy, a summer undergraduate research program of the South Carolina Space Grant Consortium.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A cartoon illustration on the general configuration of CDots and the modifying dye-derived species incorporated in the core carbon nanoparticle structure.

Absorption (ABS) and fluorescence (FLSC, 530 nm excitation) spectra of the PEG-NB@CDots in methanol (solid) and aqueous (dash-dot) solutions, and for comparison ABS of the PEG-CDots in methanol (dot).

Fluorescence quantum yields (circle, left axis) and lifetimes (diamond, right axis) of the PEG-NB@CDots in methanol at different excitation wavelengths, and for referencing absorption spectra of the solution (solid line) and the PEG-CDots in methanol (dot line).

Stern-Volmer plots for fluorescence quantum yields (circle) and lifetimes (triangle, in nanoseconds) of the PEG-NB@CDots in methanol with DNT (top) and DEA (bottom) as quenchers. The lines are from linear least square fits of the quantum yield quenching data at low quencher concentrations (0.02 M and less) and lifetime quenching data combined.

head.ce:title: Hybrid carbon dots platform enabling opportunities for desired optical properties and redox characteristics by-design

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Laboratory for Emerging Materials and Technology, Clemson University, Clemson, SC 29634, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Laboratory for Emerging Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Air Force Research Laboratory, Propulsion Directorate, Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, OH 45433, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanochemistry and Nanoengineering Group, Energy Technology and Materials Division, University of Dayton Research Institute, Dayton, OH 45469, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDots) exploit unique properties of small carbon nanoparticles, which represent the often under-appreciated zero-dimensional carbon allotrope. The carbon nanoparticles in CDots could be modified by a selected organic chromophore of desired strong red/near-IR absorption in the dot synthesis to obtain a specific type of hybrid CDots. The reported results show that these hybrid CDots derived from the modification of core carbon nanoparticles by selected dyes or other chromophoric species represent a highly versatile and effective platform for CDots of desired optical properties and photoinduced redox characteristics in a by-design fashion.

ce:section.ce:para: In the extensive pursuit of nanoscale carbon allotropes, from fullerenes at the zero-dimension to one-dimensional carbon nanotubes and more recently two-dimensional graphenes, carbon nanoparticles have largely been excluded probably for their apparent lack of the kind of structural beauty (highly symmetric, for example) found in the other carbon allotropes and/or their seemingly uninspiring properties (largely amorphous without a well defined structure, for example). However, small carbon nanoparticles, with sizes on the order of a few nanometers, should in fact be classified as another zero-dimensional carbon allotrope (in addition to fullerenes) for their unique and many superior optical, redox, and other characteristics, which have been made evident with the particle surface passivation in the nanoscale configuration now widely recognized as carbon “quantum” dots or carbon dots (CDots) [1–8].

CDots are defined generally as small carbon nanoparticles with various surface passivation schemes (Fig. 1 ) [1–3,7], and their syntheses have included primarily the deliberate chemical functionalization of pre-processed and selected small carbon nanoparticles with organic molecules [9,10] and the thermal carbonization processing of carbon-rich organic precursors [11–14]. In the latter the required surface passivation function is provided by the remaining organic species that have survived the carbonization processing and are attached to the nanoscale carbon domains [11–14]. Ever since the original finding of CDots, [1,2] there have been a large number of reported studies on all aspects of these relatively simple carbon-based nanostructures, from syntheses and structural and property characterizations [6–8] to a variety of potential applications in bioimaging and sensing, [4] optoelectronics and energy conversions, [6,8] biocidal functions, [15,16] and others [6–8,17–19]. In fact, the study and development of CDots, which again exploit the unique characteristics of a distinct zero-dimensional carbon allotrope, have emerged to become a rapidly advancing and expanding research field.

On the optical properties of CDots, the observed UV/vis absorptions are dictated by electronic transitions in the core carbon nanoparticles, which are progressively weaker toward longer wavelengths, [20] with correspondingly weaker fluorescence emissions [21]. Despite the great interest in CDots of enhanced absorption and emissions over the red/near-IR spectral region, the effort on altering the intrinsic electronic transitions in carbon nanoparticles has yielded little success. In an alternative approach, small carbon nanoparticles have been found as being compatible with modifications by organic chromophores, specifically for the incorporation of red/near-IR dyes into the nanoparticle structure to result in the desired spectroscopic enhancements over the red/near-IR spectrum in the modified CDots [22,23]. In recent literature, there have also been reports on the observation of significant red absorption and emissions in dot samples from one-pot carbonization syntheses based on some specific organic precursor mixtures [24–28]. The reported characteristics of the red emissions, including their being excitation wavelength independent, [24–28] suggest the formation of dye-like species in the carbonization processing, which would make such dot samples be conceptually similar to those from the deliberate modification with a specifically selected dye in the dot synthesis [22,23]. The dye-modified CDots (Fig. 1) may be classified as one specific type of hybrid CDots, which refer generally to carbon-based nanostructures with other elements and/or species in a CDots-like configuration [6,7,29]. What is interesting and important with this specific type of hybrid CDots, both fundamentally and technologically, was the new finding in the work reported here that the effect associated with the dye modification of the core carbon nanoparticles could go much beyond the originally anticipated host-guest relationship [22]. More specifically, the CDots with dye modification would adopt most of the dye’s absorption and fluorescence emission features, yet at the same time retain the unique photoinduced redox characteristics intrinsic to non-modified neat CDots as both excellent electron acceptors and donors [30,31]. The results suggest that the specific hybrid CDots platform based on the modification of core carbon nanoparticles by dyes or other chromophoric species may ultimately enable the preparation of CDots with desired optical and redox properties in a by-design fashion.

In the thermal carbonization synthesis of CDots from selected organic precursors, nile blue (NB) could be added for the modification purpose to yield NB@CDots, where “@” denotes “modification” broadly. In addition to the physical encapsulation of NB-derived species in the core carbon nanoparticles, [22] the modification in the nanoparticle structure by the dye species could include various other possible modes, such as doping, trapping, composition, internal structural functionalization, etc. In this work, the NB@CDots with oligomeric polyethylene glycol (PEG) for surface passivation, namely PEG-NB@CDots (Fig. 1), were synthesized in the microwave-assisted carbonization processing by following a previously reported protocol [23]. Experimentally, A mixture of NB and the oligomeric PEG in large excess was prepared and used as precursor for carbonation processing via microwave irradiation. In the processing the degree of carbonization was assessed and controlled by monitoring the relative optical absorptions at 400 nm and 550 nm, which are dominated by the carbon nanoparticles and the NB-derived species that modify the nanoparticles, respectively. Post processing the reaction mixture was cleaned and separated on an aqueous gel column to obtain the desired PEG-NB@CDots in an aqueous solution. According to transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging results, these dots are about 5 nm in average diameter [23].

Optical absorption spectra of the PEG-NB@CDots are obviously different from that of the PEG-CDots without the NB modification (Fig. 2 ), and in the former the NB-derived species must be responsible for the extra absorptions at longer wavelengths. When excited into those wavelengths, 530 nm for example, bright red fluorescence emissions from the PEG-NB@CDots were observed (Fig. 2). The quantum yields for the red emissions were found to be very high, 85–90% in a methanol solution determined by cross-referencing to three fluorescence standards, one of which was free NB in methanol with a known fluorescence quantum yield of 27% [32]. The corresponding fluorescence lifetime of the PEG-NB@CDots was also found to be long, 3.6 ns vs 1.25 ns in free NB. Such an ultrahigh fluorescence emission performance is beyond a simple explanation such that the NB-derived species become more fluorescent when confined in the more restrictive core carbon nanoparticle structure. More likely, in the modified CDots the carbon nanoparticles and the modifying dye species are not only physically incorporated, which may be considered structurally as host and guest, respectively, but also electronically interacted in such a strong fashion to result in altered or new electronic states and transitions shared by the host and guest. Thus, electronically the host-guest pair may have to be considered as one electronically integrated system instead of two segregated entities merely being together physically in a simple host-guest arrangement. Consistent with such a mechanistic picture are the negative outcomes highlighted below from experiments aimed at probing the possible presence of intra-dot host-to-guest excited state energy transfers.

For the red emission band (centered around 630 nm, Fig. 2) of the PEG-NB@CDots, observed quantum yields at a series of different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 3 . The results suggest no significant energy transfers from the photoexcited carbon nanoparticles (the host, with dominating absorptions in the blue/green) to the emissive excited states of the modifying NB-derived species (the guest), because otherwise the observed quantum yields at blue/green excitations would be much higher to be comparable with those at red excitations, which is obviously not the case as shown in Fig. 3. The fluorescence lifetime results (Fig. 3) are consistent with the assessment based on the quantum yields. The energy transfer would have to be expected if the two entities in each core carbon nanoparticle, namely the nanoscale carbon host and the guest dye species, were only physically in contact or mixed while electronically largely independent. Thus, the observed lack of excited state energy transfers suggests that the two entities must be more integrated electronically. As a relevant comparison, in a different configuration of CDots with surface tethered organic dyes, for example, substantial fluorescence resonance energy transfers (FRET) have been observed and reported, [33] because in those systems the CDots as donor and dyes as acceptor of the excited state energies are clearly two electronically largely independent entities.

The hybrid CDots as one electronically integrated entity become more evident in accordance with their photoinduced redox characteristics. As already established in the literature, [6,30,31] photoexcited CDots without dye or any other modification are in general both excellent electron acceptors and donors, with their fluorescence emissions quenched highly efficiently by electron donor and acceptor molecules, respectively. Mechanistically, the characteristic redox quenchings are attributed to the disruption of radiative recombinations of the separated elections and holes, which are generated in the ultrafast charge separation following photoexcitation of the core carbon nanoparticles in CDots [6,17]. Here in the PEG-NB@CDots, when excited into the absorption region dominated by the NB-derived species (530 nm, for example), the red emissions could also be quenched efficiently by both electron acceptor 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) and donor N,N-diethylaniline (DEA), for which the Stern-Volmer plots are shown in Fig. 4 . The slight divergence of the quenching on fluorescence quantum yields from that on fluorescence lifetimes at DNT and DEA concentrations higher than 0.02 M (Fig. 4) is likely a result of static quenching contributions even at these rather low quencher concentrations, suggesting highly effective and efficient redox interactions [34]. The Stern-Volmer constants (K SV) obtained from a linear fit on a combination of the quantum yield quenching data at quencher concentrations of 0.02 M and lower and all of the lifetime quenching data are 25 M−1 for DNT and 34 M−1 for DEA. The corresponding quenching rate constants (k q = K SV/τ F°) are 7 × 109 M−1s−1 for DNT and 1 × 1010 M−1s−1 for DEA, both at the upper limit of diffusion control. For comparison, fluorescence emissions of free NB could be quenched by DEA, but not DNT. Thus, even with the photon-harvesting due almost exclusively to the NB-derived species at the longer wavelengths (Fig. 2), the photoinduced redox characteristics of the PEG-NB@CDots is still clearly dictated by what is intrinsic to carbon nanoparticles without any modifications, the same as that in non-modified neat CDots. Again, the PEG-NB@CDots obviously behave as a single electronically integrated entity in the response to or interactions with external electron acceptors and donors.

The results presented above demonstrate that the core carbon nanoparticles in CDots could readily be modified in the nanoparticle structure with a selected organic chromophore in the carbonization synthesis, yielding likely integrated electronic structures and associated processes that are significantly different from those without the modification. With the selection of a red/near-IR dye, specifically nile blue (NB) for the PEG-NB@CDots in this work, the optical absorption for photon-harvesting is enhanced substantially over the longer wavelength spectral region in comparison with that of the neat CDots without any modification (Fig. 2), as desired and designed. The photon energies thus harvested are apparently shared by the modified CDots as a whole, not just localized in the NB-derived species, as reflected clearly in the redox quenching of the excited state energies, with the observed fluorescence quenching behavior in the PEG-NB@CDots resembling that intrinsic to non-modified neat CDots. It should be pointed out that the use of the term “NB-derived species” here is deliberate for the fact that some of the NB molecules for the modification may be damaged or structurally altered in the microwave-assisted processing, and it could be a mixture of dyes that are collectively called NB-derived species in the core carbon nanoparticles. Thus, a significant and potentially valuable feature with the modification is that it does not require the selected dye molecules to be fully intact post-processing, as long as their derived species still possess similar chromophoric characteristics as desired in the targeted spectral region.

Photoexcited CDots are known as both excellent electron acceptors and donors, but their donor function is generally somewhat better, as reflected in the more efficient fluorescence quenching by electron acceptors such as DNT [30,34]. For free nile blue, on the other hand, fluorescence emissions could be quenched by the electron donor DEA but only marginally by the acceptor DNT, suggesting the emissive excited state of an electron deficient character. Such a character could apparently be incorporated into the NB-modified CDots, with the PEG-NB@CDots exhibiting Stern-Volmer constants actually somewhat smaller for DNT than for DEA (Fig. 4). Nevertheless, the intrinsic redox characteristics of CDots as surface-passivated carbon nanoparticles still play a dominating role to have the modified CDots remain as both highly effective electron acceptors and donors.

The photoinduced redox properties of CDots are valuable to optoelectronics and related applications, as already demonstrated in some reported studies [7,35–37]. In fact, CDots may serve functions that are similar to or beyond those by fullerenes, the more beautiful and famous zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures [38,39]. For example, many uses of fullerenes in optoelectronic systems exploit their intrinsic electron acceptor characteristics and also their modifications in redox dyads and triads. CDots are equally capable electron acceptors, and at the same time electron donors as well, and they are generally more effective than fullerenes in photon-harvesting in terms of higher absorptivities on a per carbon basis. The results from this work show that the core carbon nanoparticles could be modified in the nanoparticle structure by a selected chromophore to obtain the specific type of hybrid CDots that are not only of the targeted optical absorption profiles for considerably more effective photon-harvesting but also with the redox characteristics intrinsic to CDots preserved and enhanced. Here the enhancement is such that the photoinduced electron transfers are now associated with all of the photon energies harvested by the hybrid CDots each as a single electronically integrated entity. Since there are a wide variety of selections in the chromophoric species for the modification purpose, this type of hybrid CDots represents a highly versatile and effective platform for the design and preparation of zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures with specifically targeted optical, redox, and other properties.

Hybrid CDots can be prepared facilely by modifying the core carbon nanoparticles with a chromophore of the selected optical properties, thus to allow the effective photon-harvesting across the targeted spectral region. Each of these dots behaves as a single electronically integrated entity, exhibiting the same photoinduced redox characteristics found in neat CDots even with the photoexcitation dictated by the chromophore-derived species. Thus, in this type of hybrid CDots, both their optical properties and photoinduced redox characteristics are as desired, amenable to many potential applications such as in optoelectronic and other energy conversion systems. This platform may enable property-by-design in CDots and their derived materials.

Nile blue sulfate salt was purchased from Chem-Impex International, and oligomeric polyethylene glycol (PEG, average molecular weight ∼1,000) from Alfa Aesar. N,N-diethylaniline (DEA), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), and methanol (spectroscopy grade) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Dialysis membrane tubing (molecular weight cut-off ∼1,000) was supplied by Spectrum Laboratories, and Sephadex G-100 gel by GE Healthcare Life Sciences. Water was deionized and purified in a NANOpure Water Purification System.

UV/vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Perkin-Elmer Lamba 900 spectrophotometer. Steady-state fluorescence measurements were performed on a Horiba Jobin-Yvon Fluorolog-3 (FL3-22) emission spectrometer equipped with a XBO 450 W xenon short-arc lamp and Hamamatsu T928P PMT photon counting detector operated at 950 V. Fluorescence spectra were collected in the ratio mode on the instrument and corrected for nonlinear instrument responses by applying separately determined correction factors. Fluorescence quantum yields were determined by using the relative method in cross reference to three established fluorescence dyes: 9,10-bis(phenylethynyl)-anthracene in cyclohexane, rhodamine 6G in ethanol, and nile blue in methanol with the quantum yields of unity, 95%, and 27%, respectively. Fluorescence decays were measured by using the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique on a Horibia Ultima Extreme spectrometer equipped with a SuperK Extreme supercontinuum laser source pulsed at 10 MHz, TDM-800 excitation and TDM-1200 emission monochromators, a R3809-50-MCP-PMT detector operated at 3 kV in a thermoelectrically cooled housing, and FluoroHub A++ timing electronics. The time resolution of the setup in terms of the instrument response function (IRF) is 100–250 ps, depending on excitation wavelengths. Experimental decay curves were deconvoluted by using the Das6 decay analysis software. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Hitachi H9500 high-resolution TEM system.

Nile blue (100 mg) was dissolved in ethanol (2 mL), and to the solution was added the oligomeric PEG (1 g). The resulting mixture was sonicated in a bath sonicator (VWR 250D) at 40 °C for 30 min, followed by the removal of ethanol via evaporation to obtain the reactant mixture in a small glass container. For the carbonization processing, SiC powders (170 g) in a silica crucible casting dish were heated in a conventional microwave oven at 500 W for 3 min. The small glass container with the reactant mixture was buried in the preheated SiC powders in the silica crucible casting dish for processing in the same microwave oven at 1,000 W. The initial microwave heating time was 2 min, and additional time was added in accordance with the carbonization outcome. The degree of carbonization was monitored by taking out a small quantity of the sample from the microwave oven, dispersing the sample in water, and measuring the absorbances at 400 nm (A 400nm) and 550 nm (A 550nm). The A 400nm/A 550nm ratio of about one was targeted as the end point for the microwave processing. Post-processing, the reaction mixture back at ambient temperature was dispersed in deionized water (10 mL) with sonication in a bath sonicator, and the dispersion was centrifuged at 20,000 g to keep the supernatant. The aqueous solution thus obtained was concentrated for fractionation on an aqueous Sephadex G-100 gel column to harvest the desired PEG-NB@CDots [23]. For electron microscopy characterization only, the dots were very lightly coated with silver by visible-light irradiation of the aqueous solution with a silver salt for a few minutes [20].

A solution of the dot sample in methanol was prepared. It was diluted to make solutions of the same PEG-NB@CDots concentration but varying concentrations of the quencher (DEA or DNT). Fluorescence spectra were measured with excitations at 480 nm, 530 nm, and 560 nm for relative quantum yields (ΦF°/ΦF) at different quencher concentrations. Fluorescence decays were collected with excitation wavelengths of 480 nm, 530 nm, and 560 nm and monitored emission wavelengths of 625 nm, 630 nm, and 635 nm, respectively, for the lifetime quenching (τ F°/τ F) at different quencher concentrations. The quantum yield and lifetime quenching data were used for the Stern-Volmer plots.
All authors declare no conflict of interest, financially or otherwise.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from Air Force Office of Scientific Research (AFOSR) through the program of Dr. Kenneth Caster and Air Force Research Laboratory is gratefully acknowledged. M.R. was a participant of Palmetto Academy, a summer undergraduate research program of the South Carolina Space Grant Consortium.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
